verses.text: The war between the house of Saul and the house of David drags on, but David grows steadily stronger and Saul's house weaker. God's promises do not always arrive in a single stroke; often they advance slowly, surely, until what He decreed is fully come to pass.

Sons are born to David at Hebron by several wives. The list quietly notes a growing problem: like Saul before him, David multiplies wives against God's design, and these very sons will one day fill his house with strife. Blessings misordered become sorrows in time.

Ish-bosheth accuses Abner of taking Saul's concubine — a claim to the throne — and Abner, enraged, turns on the puppet king he made. The strongman who props up a weak ruler can topple him in a day. Power built on one man's ambition is never secure.

Ish-bosheth accuses Abner of taking Saul's concubine — a claim to the throne — and Abner, enraged, turns on the puppet king he made. The strongman who props up a weak ruler can topple him in a day. Power built on one man's ambition is never secure.

Abner now vows to transfer the kingdom to David, admitting he always knew the LORD had sworn it to him. He acts at last not from faith but from wounded pride. Even God's purposes are sometimes carried forward by men whose motives are far from pure.

Abner now vows to transfer the kingdom to David, admitting he always knew the LORD had sworn it to him. He acts at last not from faith but from wounded pride. Even God's purposes are sometimes carried forward by men whose motives are far from pure.

Abner now vows to transfer the kingdom to David, admitting he always knew the LORD had sworn it to him. He acts at last not from faith but from wounded pride. Even God's purposes are sometimes carried forward by men whose motives are far from pure.

Abner sends to David, who agrees to receive him but first demands the return of Michal, his wife. David reclaims what is rightfully his and honours an old covenant. Yet the scene leaves a weeping husband behind — even just dealings can carry private grief.

Abner persuades the elders of Israel and the men of Benjamin to turn to David, reminding them the LORD had promised salvation through him. The same man who long fought God's choice now champions it. When God's time comes, even old opponents become His instruments.

Abner persuades the elders of Israel and the men of Benjamin to turn to David, reminding them the LORD had promised salvation through him. The same man who long fought God's choice now champions it. When God's time comes, even old opponents become His instruments.

Abner persuades the elders of Israel and the men of Benjamin to turn to David, reminding them the LORD had promised salvation through him. The same man who long fought God's choice now champions it. When God's time comes, even old opponents become His instruments.

Abner comes to David, who makes him a feast, and Abner pledges to gather all Israel under him; David sends him away in peace. The reunion of the kingdom is within reach, secured not by David's sword but by God turning hearts. Peace is near at last.

Abner comes to David, who makes him a feast, and Abner pledges to gather all Israel under him; David sends him away in peace. The reunion of the kingdom is within reach, secured not by David's sword but by God turning hearts. Peace is near at last.

But Joab returns from a raid, hears that Abner has come and gone in peace, and storms in to accuse him of spying. Joab's heart is set on revenge for his brother Asahel and on his own power. Suspicion and grudge will not let him see the peace within reach.

But Joab returns from a raid, hears that Abner has come and gone in peace, and storms in to accuse him of spying. Joab's heart is set on revenge for his brother Asahel and on his own power. Suspicion and grudge will not let him see the peace within reach.

Joab secretly calls Abner back and, under pretence of a quiet word, stabs him to death at the gate, avenging Asahel. A cold, treacherous murder undoes what peace had nearly won. One man's private vengeance can wreck the reconciliation of a whole nation.

Joab secretly calls Abner back and, under pretence of a quiet word, stabs him to death at the gate, avenging Asahel. A cold, treacherous murder undoes what peace had nearly won. One man's private vengeance can wreck the reconciliation of a whole nation.

David, horrified, declares himself and his kingdom forever innocent of Abner's blood and pronounces judgment on Joab's house. He will not let a murder done in his name go unmarked. A righteous leader publicly disowns the evil committed by those under him.

David, horrified, declares himself and his kingdom forever innocent of Abner's blood and pronounces judgment on Joab's house. He will not let a murder done in his name go unmarked. A righteous leader publicly disowns the evil committed by those under him.

David makes Joab and all the people mourn, follows the bier weeping, and laments, 'died Abner as a fool dieth?' He honours the fallen enemy-turned-friend with real grief. The people see the king did not desire this death, and his honest sorrow wins their trust.

David makes Joab and all the people mourn, follows the bier weeping, and laments, 'died Abner as a fool dieth?' He honours the fallen enemy-turned-friend with real grief. The people see the king did not desire this death, and his honest sorrow wins their trust.

David fasts till sunset, and all the people are pleased, knowing his grief is sincere; he calls Abner 'a prince and a great man.' Yet he confesses himself weak against the ruthless sons of Zeruiah, leaving their reward to God. Even a king must sometimes wait for God to judge the violent.
